sentences.text: Oh, I, I wouldn't be able to answer you that.
All I knew is my parents sewed it onto my clothes.
And whether we could buy it or they distributed or, or Jewish organization gave it to us, I wouldn't know.
I just can't comment any further.
All I knew is that when I left home, I had the Jewish stars on, on me.
And it didn't last very long, because at-- according to my knowledge, we started wearing when the Germans came in.
And they came in Apr-- in March.
And I left Hungary in July, so I only wore it a few months.
And when I got my pass to work outside, I didn't have to wear it.
So I didn't wear it.
Only inside the given area where Jews could move in those days.
I know my, my father was tremendously affected by it, because he was a, a socialist from the beginning, as far as I can remember.
And to him, freedom meant a lot.
And he felt like he'd been stamped like a cattle with the stars.
But, as I said before, they did not convey they feelings or opinion about the area to the children.
I only know about my own experience.
I know when the first time we went out with the Jewish stars, the feeling was unbelievably-- it is not embarrassed, because when you are a child, you are not embarrassed by anything.
But it was a, a, a scary, scary feeling.
People looked at you like you come from Mars or something.
And, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's about all I can say in, in the, the, the Jewish star.
I couldn't comment any more.
Sure.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in practically every suburbs, you had a synagogue.
And according to, to my hearings, to my knowledge, it's over 150,000, 200,000 Jews lived in, in Hungary.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the majority was executed mostly-- according, again, to my-- to what I heard, because I wasn't in Hungary when the real atrocities begun.
They, they started killing Jews fair dinkum when the Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you could call-- it wasn't the hakenkruis.
It was a different kruis.
It was the Arrows, the, the Four Arrows.
They were the Hungarian fascist parties.
And they started collecting Jews and shoot them in the Danube.
But by the time when this started, I, I was a long time ago already in Germany doing my own things with the Germans.
Yes.
Certain houses where not too many Jews lived, they moved the Jews out.
And they moved into buildings where a lot of Jews lived.
So they made Jewish houses.

Most probably, according to my knowledge today, they did that because they wanted to concentrate Jews into buildings so later on, if they deporting them, they, they are much easier to get them than when they are scattered around and mixed up.
But at the moment, I have a non-Jewish friend here from Hungary whom I met just here in Ha-Koah Club.
And he told me answering your questions that opposite us was also a Jewish house where only a few non-Jews lived.
And they had sign in they doors-- on they doors, that in this flat, non-Jews are living, which I didn't know those time, because that time we lived-- I lived in a, a, a house where only Jews were.
So we didn't know what the Christians were doing, how they live or what they doing or whatever.
So it must have been houses where still was a few.
But they were definitely marked, because I think this guy, [INAUDIBLE], he said that I remember that I lived in a Jewish house.
Actually, he's a funny man.
His mother was Jewish, his father Christian.
And according to the Jewish faith, whoever has a Jewish mother is a Jew.
But during the war, his, his-- her husband sheltered her such a way that nobody knew that she is Jewish.
So all the boys grown up as Christians.
And even to-- up till today, they lived and they behaved and they thought as Christian, even having a Jewish mother.
But answering to your, your question that-- all we had to do is we could take our belongings, but they are located a flat in a building where mostly Jews lived already.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we just had to move there.
And, and because it was more Jews than flats available, we lived with two or three families, I'm not sure now-- it was a, a, a two-bedroom flat, I think.
So it was three families lived in that flat.
And by that time, our schooling became very, very fragile.
First of all, the schools didn't operate at 100%, because there was no coal available to, to heat the schools.
So our schooling were interrupted.
And later on, as a Jew, I couldn't go to school.
So as a youngster, we mostly stayed at home and moved inside an area which was allocated for Jews.
Oh, yes.
We did, yes.

We had to mix, because we ate in the same table, because by that time, it was my parents and other adults lived in, in the same bedroom between-- two set of parents lived in one bedroom, and the third set of parents lived in, in the lunchroom, including the children.
And they cooked in the kitchen together.
When I said to you that we were a middle class family before all these things started, we had a bath about three times a week because we had our own bathroom.
In those days, that meant you were rich.
And we had the showers and we had hot water system.
But when the moving started from our home to a Jewish house, all this stopped.
And, and sometimes I was having not a bath, but having a dish full of water and wash myself and a lady, half naked, one of the mothers, like, she was having a wash next to me in another dish.
So the, the the-- the drop of living standard, it's-- it immediately arised.
And from then on, I knew I was a Jew.
What my parents did, they still had the shop.
So they still could go to the shop, but they only could go with their stars.
And the shop was also marked as a Jewish shop.
By that time, we couldn't get poultry, because Jews wasn't allowed to sell poultry.
But we were allowed to sell fruit, like apples, and oranges, and fruits.
So they opened a fruit shop about 500, 600 meters from the market in a-- in a closed shop, not an open market.
And they went there every day exactly the same way.
But I know my father-- now my memory is a little bit came back immediately.
My father was forced into forced labor camp.
But as children, we only knew that he disappeared for, for a few months, sometimes for a month, sometimes two months, three months.
And we knew that they took the fathers somewhere, but we didn't know where.
Then he came home on leave and he was not wearing uniform.
He was wearing high boots and, and was full of mud and dirty.
And they told us that he is in a-- in a forced labor camp.
Not a labor camp, they were forced labor.
Yeah, I think so.
It was a forced labor camp.
They took them to 300, 400 kilometers away-- I'm sorry, you are already overseas then.
It only took you about 150, 160 kilometers to little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] country towns.
And they were shoveling and, and doing whatever they do.
I don't know, because as I said, we children didn't-- we wasn't-- we didn't have to know everything, and we didn't know.
Wandering around, go to the, to the allocated playground, and more or less just, just wandering around.
School, we went to school when school was on.
When we came home from school, we had to go-- come home right away.
And we couldn't play with other kids.
We had to play in the allocated place where the Jewish house was.
And that was it.
Old friends didn't appear anymore, Christian friends.
The Jewish friends, we established new people, whoever were in the house, because mostly your life is centralized around the area where you lived.
The, the movement of yours been restricted so much that you only could move in, in the area where, where you could-- where you were allowed to move.
Yes.
Yes.

Look, we had radio, and we had what you would call-- what we call the Voice of America, which you only could listen in a very, very quiet manner, because if some, somebody heard that you were listening, you were prosecuted.
And prosec-- by prosecution, I don't know what I mean.
I don't know whether they'd been taken away or they'd been fined.
I was too young to know.
But I knew, because I know from London even the, the, the signal of the station, the [SINGING] And when this came, we knew that we listening something which we are not supposed to listen.
And it came through the Hungarian section of the London BBC.
And we knew what happening around as much as you could hear on the radio.
I mean, newspapers was fascist newspapers.
I mean, it was Hungarian papers, which was governed by, those days, government.
So they put into the paper whatever they want you to, to know and not what actually happened.
You didn't hear much about deportation, concentration camps.
All we heard is that, as I mentioned to you before, that young children came to our home to eat whose parents had been taken away, nobody knew where.
Because when I, I was picked up and I was taken to Auschwitz, I didn't know where I was either.
All I see on the lager, the head [INAUDIBLE] is 'Arbeit-- arbeit-- Arbeit macht frei.' And with my little knowledge, I know that 'works make you free.' And that was beautiful.
That was a slogan which stayed with me for the rest of my life.
No.
I tell you frankly, no.
Even if I remember, I didn't give too much.
I didn't put too much attention, because I was a child.
I mean, I was 11, 12.
You don't listen radios.
You're kicking balls around and stones and whatever comes to your feet.
You don't listen to radios.
The food?
You could buy.
With fruit we had no problem, because we had-- my parents had fruit.
And I feel that my parents had still some connection with the previous people, because we had meat, goose, which was the everyday food in those days.
We had flours, and we had fat, because you-- those days you cook with fat.
And, and we had, with food, I can't remember that I went hungry.
If I would say yes, I would be a liar.
Maybe my parents was-- were smart people or maybe their connection, because of the food industry they'd been in all they lives, was better than other people.
We never went hungry.
Yes, that's right.
That's right.
And the other people has also-- you see, you have to know one thing, that in Hungary-- I don't know other parts of Europe.
In Hungary, every house had a, a, a cool room, what we called Speis.
This was a German word.
It was a cool room where you always stored up food for practically a year.
If they-- if they brought a goose home, they, they grilled-- actually, they cooked the fat, which went into jars.
Then in the summertimes, they made cucumbers in, in, in glasses.
And they made red paprika in glasses.
And they bought big pieces of speck, which is a side of a, of a, a, a cow, and they store it.
So every family, when they moved from that building to the Jewish house, took with them whatever food they prepared.
So more or less, the, the cool room, particularly when three family lived in it, more or less was filled up.
So we got all whatever we got from there.
And, and they were-- we were well fed.
We didn't have any problem as far as food concerns.
Nothing I know of.
But if you left with a David star, you went out, you got apprehended.
Later on what happened, I wouldn't know.
Because when the, the real ghetto was established, I was already in Germany.
So my recollection of Hungary of that time, is, is, is very limited.
My, my war years happened in Germany.
Yes.
In-- it was a place called [INAUDIBLE]-- [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know the number.
That was-- where was the, the Jewish committee, which was established by the Germans to be a, a-- an in-between organization between the Germans and the Jewish people.
They were giving us the order.
What the German gave orders, they gave to the Jewish committee, and they executed the order to us.
So we did not need the, the German, neither the Gestapo in my time or whoever, whoever ruled Budapest.
But they gave the instruction to these people.
And I was called in as a-- as a youngster by the Jewish community to [INAUDIBLE] to volunteer.
They said to you, it's better volunteer than you being picked.
Volunteer to, to do some work, and I was very happy.
They said you get a pass.
When you go outside, you don't have to wear the Jewish star, and you can move around a little bit freer than your parents.
In those days, to me, that meant I'm a big man.
And-- and they-- With me, another 40 or 50 young guys, a few of them friend of mine, a few of them from the orphanage of Budapest, they established a, a, a tiny little commando.
And we went out a place called Nagyteteny, which is about 15, 18 kilometers from Budapest.
And we were employed by the Hungarian Road Building Ministerium, the road department here.
And our job was, they give us-- they took us from the [NON-ENGLISH] by truck out to the site.
And we had to shovel the roads.
We had to dig out the roads.
And then they put stones on it, and then we had to level the stones and then put sand on it.
So we were working actually as, as road builders.
And we went there in the morning and came home in the night.
And to me, that was beautiful, because I was out from, from the, from the allocated area.
I could move.
And when the bombardment came to Budapest, from Nagyteteny, we could see the planes.
We could hear the air raids, the sirens.
We could see the planes coming because we were up in the-- in the little mountainous area.
And as youngsters, we watched the planes.
We didn't know the significance so much of the bombing, although we'd been under the buildings in the last three, four years quite many times when the air raid signal came.
But that was-- everybody had to go down, because the bomb didn't discriminate.
And so we watched with child eyes how the Americans and the Englishmen bombarded Budapest.
So when, when this happened, after the bombardments, they stopped us going into the, the road work.

They took us to factories, which I can't remember name, where we have to-- had to clean up the, the rubbles and, and sweep and, and, and try to-- we didn't have the right to interfere with the-- with either the Red Cross or, or the ambulance services or the fire brigade.
But after everybody left, we cleaned up whatever rubble that is left after bombardment.
And when, when they didn't needed us, we went out again, to do the roads.
And, and that was it.
And one day, a truck came.
We thought we're going home early.
But the truck was loaded with-- oh, I could say around ten Gendarmes.
The Gendarmes were a special Hungarian force which had a feather on their hat, and they were very feared by Jews because they were a very, very anti-Semitic organization.
They were the policeman in the country, not in the capital city.
Budapest had a police force.
The, the country had these Gendarmes.
And they came, not the police.
And all they did, they put us onto the trucks.
And from Nagyteteny, they took us into Budateteny into a school.
Budateteny was just a next little village.
And into the school came one of these Gendarmes who was, according to my estimation, a lieutenant.
And the rest of them was just the soldiers.
And in the school, strange things happened.
He started interrogate us one by one.
And when the guys came out from the interrogation room, they looked a little bit untidy.
And as a youngster, I didn't have any knowledge what's going on.
When I got in, then I realized actually what's happening.
He called me to the desk he was sitting, and he moved his chair, sitting position, and asked me to come close.
And then he unbuttoned my fly and started playing.

And while he was playing with us-- he was-- with me, actually-- he started asking questions, whether my parents had been in the resistance movement, or, or do we have anything to hide, like papers or, or valuables or whatever it is.
And while he was asking the question, he was playing.
And I knew what he was doing, because, as I said, I'd been grown up in the street.
And I knowed that he's a homosexual.
But I never had encounter, actually, in my time, which is very different way of thinking.
We made fun of homosexuals if we knew, because on the market, it was toilets.
And on the toilets in the evening, these people are gathered.
But those days, was against the law, so they were very low profile.
So I couldn't tell him anything about my parents, because I never knew that my parents had anything to do with, with any organization which was resistance, or whatever.
I didn't know anything about our wealth, because we'd never been informed.
My parents never told me what we have, what we haven't got.
And when this finished, I went out, and the next guy came in.
And as children, we were absolutely mesmerized, what happened to us.
And I might be-- I might been very young, but, thanks God, that was my first and last experience of that kind.
That it, it didn't leave a mark on me, but I, I remember it like-- I remember the face of the bastard.
So when the-- all the interrogation was over, they put us on a truck.
And the truck went down to the side of the Danube, what we called the, the rock part.
That's the, the place where they are loading, the loading docks.
Sorry, the loading dock down in the Danube.
And that was about, I remember well, 2 o'clock, because we had to wait till dark until a boat came.
And they put us on the boat.
And the boat went down, and we arrived to called Budakalasz, a brickwork.
That was an unused brickwork where all the Jewish people been assembled from all the area.
I don't know where the people came from, where they'd been apprehended.
But it was a lot of people there.
And the minute we got out of the truck, they put us into the, into the brickwork.
And we were flabbergasted, because we didn't know where we were.
All we, we-- I remember vividly the stench.
You could smell death because people there were longer than we were, and people died there.
We, we slept inside the caves because they have caves where they, they-- where the bricks are cooling off after the burning period.
And we slept there with the clothes on-- no blankets, no nothing-- on the ground, or whatever you could find place.
And we were squashed in like herrings in, in that-- int that assemble lager, I couldn't call it concentration lager.
It was a assembly lager, where all the Jews from the areas-- or from other areas, I don't know.
And that was on Monday.
I got picked up on Monday, the 3rd of July.
In the truck, yeah.
They were together with me.
How many?
It's about 40-- 40, 45.
And in the same night, we arrive to that.
And that makes, let's say, Tuesday morning.
And from Tuesday morning, we scattered out a little bit, the 40 boys.
I was with a childhood friend of mine who never came back.
Mengele pulled us apart.
He went to the left, I went to the right.
I will tell that about later.
And about another four or five friends of mine who we got friendly with the-- with the orphanage boys, we stayed in one corner, and all the other boys been scattered around.
And we existed there till Thursday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Existence means that you didn't have to do anything.
You just walked around, talked.
Adults didn't talk much about it, because we were very little.
They talked amongst each other.
We heard something.
But anyway, in Thursday, the brickwork had a, a, a railway, because the brick had to be delivered from somewhere.
So they had a railway line, and they brought in a, a, a whole set of freight carriages, which is-- in Hungary meant cows, sheep, whatever it is.
And they put us in till was full, till seams.
I think so.
That's the correct word.
And they locked up the wagons.
Came the locomotive, and my trip to Auschwitz begun.
Which meant that they only opened in [INAUDIBLE], which is already in Czechoslovakia-- no food, no water, no nothing.
And in [INAUDIBLE], they already took out about six or seven elderly people who were just about dying.
Some of them went from thirst, some of them went banana in the wagon, which means they went cuckoo, they went mad.
So they took them off.
And I never forgot the guy who gave us to the Germans.
And that was one of the head Gendarmes in Hungary.
He was the boss of this Gendarme organization.
And if I'm not wrong, his name was [?
Ferenczy, ?] because somebody said-- one of us said to us that, have a look.
This, this must be [?
Ferenczy, ?] because he had a lot of gold in here and a lot of gold in there and bigger feather than the rest of them.
He, he seems like the, the head man.
And after the war, when I came back, we had a connection with the-- with the Hungarian authorities after the war.
And I, I was present of the hanging and the-- and the legal proceedings when these war criminals was brought to justice.
And I seen that guy.
I was on his [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] trial.
And they said his name was [?
Ferenczy. ?] So he was the guy who gave our troop into the Germans.
Because from then-- until then, the Gendarmes, these people were guarding us.
Because wherever we stopped, we heard.
They didn't open the wagon, but we heard Hungarian talking.
After we left [INAUDIBLE], we heard only German.
So we knew that we had been taken over by the Germans.
